title: How memory actually works
description: Encode, store, retrieve — and how to make each one stick.
summaryPoints: Encode with meaning — connect new stuff to what you already know, not blind repetition
Working memory holds only ~4–7 things at once, so chunk and rehearse to move it to long-term
Quiz yourself: the act of recalling is what makes a memory stronger
Beat the forgetting curve with spaced reviews at expanding intervals (day 1, 3, 7, 30)
Stack the boosters — chunking, mnemonics, sleep, and words-plus-visuals

content.title: Three doors, not one
content.body:
You don't have one "memory" — you have a tiny assembly line with three stations.

- **Encode** — getting info in
- **Store** — holding onto it
- **Retrieve** — pulling it back out when you need it

Most "I have a bad memory" moments are really just one of these three stations being skipped. The good news: you can train all three.
content.imageAlt: Encode, store, and retrieve arrows feeding into a box

content.title: Encoding runs on meaning
content.body:
Reading a line ten times feels like studying, but your brain barely notices it. That's shallow encoding.

Deep encoding sticks because you give the info a **meaning**: link it to something you already know, find an example, or picture it in your head.

"Mitochondria make energy" is forgettable. "Mitochondria are the phone charger of the cell" hooks onto something real — so it stays.

content.prompt: Which study move encodes the deepest?
content.context: You're learning a new term for biology.
options: Reading the definition out loud five times in a row
Connecting the term to your own example and picturing it
Highlighting the whole paragraph in yellow
Copying the definition word-for-word into your notes
content.explanation: Tying new info to something you already know and visualizing it is deep encoding — repetition and highlighting are shallow and fade fast.

content.prompt: Match each memory station to its job.

content.title: Your mental sticky note
content.body:
Working memory — the stuff you're holding right now — is shockingly small. It fits only about **4 to 7 items**, and only for a few seconds.

That's why a phone number flies out of your head the moment someone distracts you.

To keep something, you have to move it into long-term storage — and that happens through meaning and rehearsal, not by staring harder.

content.prompt: Your working memory can hold almost unlimited items at once.
content.explanation: Working memory is tiny — roughly 4 to 7 items for a few seconds. Anything more has to be chunked or moved into long-term memory.

content.prompt: Put memory's three stations in the order info travels through them.
content.explanation: Info has to get in (encode), be held (store), and only then can you pull it back out (retrieve). Skip the first and there's nothing to find.

content.prompt: Grouping a number like 5-5-5-1-2-3-4 into 555-1234 is called ___.
content.hint: Phone numbers are split into groups on purpose.
content.explanation: Chunking groups loose items into bigger units, so seven digits feel like two pieces — beating the limits of working memory.

content.title: Retrieval is the workout
content.body:
Here's the twist most people miss: **the act of recalling a memory is what makes it stronger.**

Re-reading your notes feels productive but barely lifts anything. Closing the notes and trying to remember — that's the rep that builds the muscle.

This is why testing yourself beats reviewing. Good cues help too: a keyword or image gives your brain a handle to grab the memory by.
content.imageAlt: A brain lit up with a spark of activity

content.prompt: What actually makes a memory stronger?
options: Reading the same notes one more time
The effort of recalling it from memory
Keeping the notes open while you study
content.explanation: Retrieval practice — pulling the answer from your own head — strengthens the memory each time. Passive re-reading mostly feels easy without helping.

content.instructions: One word makes this false. Tap it.
content.sentence: Quizzing yourself weakens a memory every single time you recall it.
content.explanation: Each successful recall strengthens the memory — that's the whole power of retrieval practice.

content.title: The forgetting curve
content.body:
Learn something once and walk away, and it drains out fast: you forget roughly **half within an hour** and most of it within a day.

But every time you review, the curve flattens — you forget more slowly after each pass.

So forgetting isn't a character flaw. It's the default. Reviewing on a schedule is how you fight back.
content.imageAlt: A memory decay curve lifted higher by review spikes

content.prompt: After learning something once with no review, about what % is gone within a day?
content.explanation: Without review you lose most of it within a day — around 70%. Each review flattens that drop so you keep more.

content.prompt: Does this strengthen or weaken your memory?

items.text: Connecting it to a vivid example

content.title: Stack the boosters
content.body:
A few cheap tricks make all three stations work better:

- **Chunking** — group items, like a phone number
- **Mnemonics** — acronyms, silly stories, or a memory palace for random lists
- **Sleep** — your brain consolidates the day's learning while you're out
- **Dual coding** — pair words with a picture so you store it two ways

None of these are talent. They're just better defaults.

content.prompt: You have to memorize an arbitrary list of 8 terms for tomorrow. Best tool?
options: A mnemonic like an acronym or a story linking them
Reading the list straight through 20 times
Pulling an all-nighter to fit in more passes
content.explanation: Mnemonics give arbitrary items a structure to hang on, and sleep beats an all-nighter because it's when memories consolidate.

content.prompt: You learned today's lecture. When should you review to beat forgetting?
content.explanation: Expanding intervals — day 0, 1, 3, 7 — refresh the memory right before you'd forget. One late cram can't undo two weeks of forgetting.
